annotations.label: Regular and singular operators

anchor.snippet: If the inverse exists, T is called regular .

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named 'regular/singular operator' definition, but the underlying notion of invertibility (IsUnit of ζ·1−T) is captured by resolventSet/spectrum membership.

annotations.label: Resolvent set

anchor.snippet: the resolvent set of T is the set of all complex numbers ζ such that

annotations.note: `resolventSet R a` is defined as the set of r where `algebraMap r - a` is a unit, exactly the resolvent set.

annotations.label: Spectrum of an operator

anchor.snippet: The spectrum of T is the set of all complex numbers ζ such that

annotations.note: `spectrum R a` is defined as the complement of the resolvent set, i.e. the ζ for which ζ·1−a is not invertible.

annotations.label: Resolvent

anchor.snippet: is called the resolvent of T

annotations.note: `resolvent a r = (algebraMap r - a)⁻¹ʳ` is the resolvent function of an algebra element.

annotations.label: Eigenvalues belong to the spectrum

anchor.snippet: Every eigenvalue of T belongs to σ ( T ), but σ ( T ) may contain non-eigenvalues.

annotations.note: `HasEigenvalue.mem_spectrum` proves that any eigenvalue of an endomorphism lies in its spectrum.

annotations.label: Spectrum of self-adjoint operators

anchor.snippet: for self-adjoint operators , the spectrum lies on the real line

annotations.note: `IsSelfAdjoint.mem_spectrum_eq_re` shows every spectral value of a self-adjoint element equals its real part, so the spectrum is real.

annotations.label: Operator as a dyadic product

anchor.snippet: a very particular linear operator L might be written as a dyadic product

annotations.note: `InnerProductSpace.rankOne 𝕜 x y` is the rank-one (outer/dyadic product) operator |x⟩⟨y|, with `rankOne_apply` giving z ↦ ⟪y,z⟫•x.

annotations.label: Eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of L

anchor.snippet: are eigenvalues of L and the functions

annotations.note: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a linear operator are formalized as `Module.End.HasEigenvalue` and `Module.End.HasEigenvector`.

annotations.label: Resolution of the identity

anchor.snippet: This expression of the identity operation is called a representation or a resolution of the identity.

annotations.note: `OrthonormalBasis.sum_rankOne_eq_id` states ∑ᵢ |eᵢ⟩⟨eᵢ| = id, the resolution of the identity for a finite orthonormal basis.

annotations.label: Generalized Fourier expansion

anchor.snippet: which is the generalized Fourier expansion of ψ in terms of the basis functions

annotations.note: `OrthonormalBasis.sum_repr'` gives ψ = ∑ᵢ ⟪eᵢ, ψ⟫ • eᵢ, the generalized Fourier expansion (also `HilbertBasis.hasSum_repr` for the infinite case).

annotations.label: Dyad expansion of L

anchor.snippet: Then the resolution of the identity above provides the dyad expansion of L

annotations.note: The spectral/diagonalization theorem is formalized via the eigenvector basis, but the explicit dyad expansion L = ∑ λᵢ |eᵢ⟩⟨eᵢ| is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Resolvent poles at eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: This function has poles in the complex λ -plane at each eigenvalue of L .

annotations.note: The meromorphic structure of the resolvent (poles located at eigenvalues) is not formalized in Mathlib, only the resolvent function itself.

annotations.label: Green's function for L

anchor.snippet: is called the Green's function for operator L

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Green's-function construction for (differential) operators.

annotations.label: Solution of operator equation via Green's function

anchor.snippet: the function ψ ( x ) satisfying the operator equation is found if we can find the spectrum of O , and construct G

annotations.note: Solving an operator equation by constructing a Green's function from the spectrum is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximizer of Rayleigh quotient is largest eigenvector

anchor.snippet: Let M be a symmetric matrix and let x be the non-zero vector that maximizes the Rayleigh quotient with respect to M

annotations.note: `IsSelfAdjoint.hasEigenvector_of_isMaxOn` proves a maximizer of the Rayleigh quotient of a self-adjoint operator is an eigenvector with eigenvalue the supremum, generalizing the symmetric-matrix statement.
